---
title: "Stock Recruit Model"
author: "Josh Korman"
output:
  html_document: default
  theme: flatly
vignette: "%\\VignetteIndexEntry{Stock Recruit Model} %\\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}\n"
#output: word_document
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  fig.width = 7,
  fig.height = 5
)
```

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyverse)
library(bayesplot)
library(wesanderson)
library(SRJPEdata)
library(SRJPEmodel)
```

## Overview

Freshwater stock-recruitment models with covariates estimate annual outmigrant abundance as a function of spawner abundance and covariates. The models are fit to historical estimates of abundance and covariate conditions. These relationships can then be used to make forecasts of outmigrant abundance in the JPE model. 

## Model Objective

Spawner to recruit relationships can be used to make forecasts of outmigrant abundance in the JPE model. 

## Conceptual model

The stock recruit model takes in adult abuncace estimates, juvenile abundance estimates, and covariates to forecast outmigrant abundance. See figure 1 below to see how it fits into the full JPE model system.

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = "500px"}
knitr::include_graphics(path = here::here("vignettes", "images", "SR jpe diagram.png"), rel_path = FALSE)
```

*Figure 1: Conceptual Diagram of SRJPE model. Stock recruit model shown in yellow.* 

# Model Architecture 

Spawner abundances used in the fitting can be direct annual observations of total redds (from redd surveys) or spawners (from holding or carcass surveys), or from models that predict spawner abundance based on passage abundance (the P2S model).

```{r, echo = FALSE, out.width = "500px"}
knitr::include_graphics(path = here::here("vignettes", "images", "SR_conceptual_diagram.png"), rel_path = FALSE)
```

*Figure 2: Conceptual Diagram of Stock Recruit model.* 


Water year type, discharge, or water temperature will be used as covariates in the modeling. Covariates consist of annual values that will capture effects of environmental conditions on survival rates for different components of early life history between spawning and outmigration (e.g., water temperature during incubation, emergence, or early rearing). Owing to limitations in sample size (number of years available) and uncertainty in stock (spawner) and recruitment (outmigrant abundance) estimates, we anticipate estimating single-covariate models only. An information theoretic approach will be used to identify the most predictive models. We will develop tributary-specific stock-recruitment models as well as models for the mainstem traps based on the sum of spawner abundances in tributaries upstream of the mainstem traps.

# Model Inputs

The model takes in one dataset produced by `prepare_stock_recruit_inputs()` function. This function takes in a database connection to the model run database, a stream, an adult data type (either passage, redd, holding, broodstock_tag, or carcass), the covariate you would like to use to fit the model (see     `?prepare_stock_recruit_inputs()` for the full list of covariates), and a booleen value indicating if you would like to truncate the dataset to only include years where there are data for all covariates. 

# Running stock recruit submodel

The model is run for a given stream, adult data type, and environmental covariate.
 
```{r, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE, results='hide'}
# run the model for battle creek and water year type
cfg <- config::get()

con <- DBI::dbConnect(RPostgres::Postgres(),
                      dbname = cfg$db_name,
                      host = cfg$db_host,
                      port = cfg$db_port,
                      user = cfg$db_user,
                      password = cfg$db_password)

battle_inputs <- prepare_stock_recruit_inputs(con = con, 
                                              stream = "battle creek", 
                                              adult_data_type = "redd", 
                                              covariate =  "rearing_max_flow",
                                              truncate_dataset = FALSE)

battle_SR_model <- fit_stock_recruit_model(battle_inputs)
```

# Model Output
The Stock Recruit model produces a STAN fit object when run. You can extract parameter estimates from the model object with the function `extract_stock_recruit_estimates()`. This produces a table.

```{r, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
battle_parameter_estimates <- extract_stock_recruit_estimates(
  stock_recruit_inputs = battle_inputs, 
  stock_recruit_model_object = battle_SR_model)
```

```{r, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
knitr::kable(head(battle_parameter_estimates, 10))
```

The following plots show SR model results. 

```{r, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
SRJPEmodel::generate_diagnostic_plot_sr(sr_inputs = battle_inputs, 
                                        sr_fit = battle_SR_model)

SRJPEmodel::generate_diagnostic_plot_sr_covar(sr_inputs = battle_inputs, 
                                        sr_fit = battle_SR_model)

generate_results_plot_sr(sr_inputs = battle_inputs, 
                         con = con)

```

**Resources**
For more in depth documentation on the Spring Run JPE Stock Recruit Model please see the full report.
